title: General Fund Reserve Policy
adopted_by: Ayes: Bartlett, Davila, Droste, Hahn, Maio, Wengraf, Worthington and Arreguin. Noes: None. Absent: None.
last_amended_note: Resolution No. 71-908-N.S. amended the target level from fixed dollar/percentage milestones to 20-30% of GF revenues. See acfr_fy2025.json.

key_provisions.purpose: Prepare for the impact of economic cycles and catastrophic events. Assure fluctuations in revenue do not impede the City's ability to meet expenditure obligations.

reserve_structure.stability_reserve_purpose: Mitigate revenue shortfalls during a single fiscal year or prolonged recession. Not for new programs or projects.
reserve_structure.catastrophic_reserve_purpose: Sustain operations during public emergencies — earthquakes, fires, floods, civil unrest, terrorist attacks. NOT for operational shortfalls or new programs.

withdrawal_requirement.verbatim: General Fund reserves, upon a two-thirds vote of the City Council, may be used in accordance with the standards set forth herein.
withdrawal_requirement.scoring_significance: The workers' compensation fund transfer ($4.7M FY2025, $5.2M FY2026) and Section 115 Trust drawdowns are not drawn from the Stability or Catastrophic Reserve — they come from separate internal service fund balances. The 2/3 supermajority requirement applies to the named reserves; these other transfers appear to have been authorized through the budget process, not through the reserve withdrawal mechanism.

original_targets.intermediate: 16.7% by end of FY2020 'if financially feasible'
original_targets.long_term: 30% of GF revenues within no more than 10 years (by approximately 2027)
original_targets.funding_mechanism: Starting FY2018, 50% of excess equity above first $1M allocated to reserves; additional excess equity by majority vote

replenishment_policy.replenishment_schedule: City Manager must recommend replenishment schedule simultaneous with any withdrawal appropriation (or within 3 months if emergency)
replenishment_policy.repayment_start: No more than 5 years from date of withdrawal
replenishment_policy.repayment_complete: Within 10 years from date of withdrawal

key_provisions.prohibited_uses: Reserve shall NOT be used for ongoing or new programs or services.

july_2025_amendment.change: Target level changed from fixed milestones (30% by 2027) to a range of 20-30% of GF revenues.
july_2025_amendment.scoring_significance: At $58.3M reserves against $285.8M revenues, the city holds 20.4% — barely above the new 20% floor. The original 2017 policy targeted 30% by 2027 ($85.7M at current revenue). The council is $27M short of the original target but amended the policy so that what they hold is now compliant. The amendment was adopted just after FY2025 ended.

scoring_implications: The original 2017 reserve policy set a 30% of GF revenues target by 2027. The council amended it in July 2025 to 20-30%, converting non-compliance into compliance without adding to reserves.

The 2/3 supermajority requirement for reserve withdrawals is a meaningful governance protection — but it applies to the named Stability and Catastrophic Reserves. The workers' comp and Section 115 transfers that the City Auditor flagged as one-time measures may have been authorized through the budget process under a different mechanism.

The replenishment policy requires a replenishment schedule at the time of any withdrawal. If the workers' comp and IT fund transfers were treated as reserve withdrawals, a replenishment schedule would be required. If they were treated as budget line items, no replenishment obligation attaches.
The Catastrophic Reserve policy explicitly prohibits use 'to meet operational shortfalls.' Using any portion of reserves for recurring salary or benefit costs would violate this prohibition.
